coredata.dc:title: The cavitation erosion of ultrasonic sonotrode during large-scale metallic casting: Experiment and simulation
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Yang
Liu, Zhilin
Li, Xiaoqian
Zhang, Lihua
Li, Ruiqing
Jiang, Ripeng
Dong, Fang
coredata.dc:description: Ultrasonic sonotrodes play an essential role in transmitting power ultrasound into the large-scale metallic casting. However, cavitation erosion considerably impairs the in-service performance of ultrasonic sonotrodes, leading to marginal microstructural refinement. In this work, the cavitation erosion behaviour of ultrasonic sonotrodes in large-scale castings was explored using the industry-level experiments of Al alloy cylindrical ingots (i.e. 630 mm in diameter and 6000 mm in length). When introducing power ultrasound, severe cavitation erosion was found to reproducibly occur at some specific positions on ultrasonic sonotrodes. However, there is no cavitation erosion present on the ultrasonic sonotrodes that were not driven by electric generator. Vibratory examination showed cavitation erosion depended on the vibration state of ultrasonic sonotrodes. Moreover, a finite element (FE) model was developed to simulate the evolution and distribution of acoustic pressure in 3-D solidification volume. FE simulation results confirmed that significant dynamic interaction between sonotrodes and melts only happened at some specific positions corresponding to severe cavitation erosion. This work will allow for developing more advanced ultrasonic sonotrodes with better cavitation erosion-resistance, in particular for large-scale castings, from the perspectives of ultrasonic physics and mechanical design.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasonic sonotrode
Power ultrasound
Cavitation erosion
Numerical simulation
Large-scale casting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cavitation erosion locations on ultrasonic sonotrodes
Comparation between ambient and casting conditions

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the ultrasound-assisted casting system for large-scale ingots, including 1. Hot-top, 2. Ultrasonic sonotrode, 3. 3-D volume for solidification, 4. Launder, 5. Crystallizer, 6. Cooling water, 7. Mushy zone, 8. Pulling device, and 9. Solid ingot. (b) and (c) are the industry-level casting apparatus for large-scale casting. As a reference for ultrasonic sonotrode I, the ultrasonic sonotrode II is not driven by electric generator.

(a) one large-scale cast 2A14 Al alloy ingot, micrographs of the large-scale cast 2A14 Al alloy ingot treated (b) with and (c) without power ultrasound, and (b1)-(b3) and (c1)-(c3) indicate corresponding microstructures from (b) and (c), respectively.
Geometrical illustration of the finite element (FE) model, showing how a single ultrasonic sonotrode was configured in casting.

Experimental setup for vibratory examination of ultrasonic sonotrodes, showing how to determine the vibration amplitudes along the longitudinal and transversal directions of ultrasonic sonotrodes, respectively.

Ultrasonic sonotrodes with and without being driven by electric generator: (a) a new ultrasonic sonotrode with clean surface, (b) an immersed ultrasonic sonotrode without cavitation erosion, and (c)-(d) two immersed ultrasonic sonotrodes with severe cavitation erosion. As a reference, the ultrasonic sonotrode in (b) was only immersed in the 2A14 Al alloy melts without being driven by electric generator. Ultrasonic sonotrodes in (c) and (d) were driven by electric generator.

Representative SEM micrographs collected from the cavitation erosion positions, including (a) a large and (b) a close-up areas of ultrasonic sonotrode surfaces. Numerical cavitation erosion-produced Al3Ti particles are highlighted by the white dashed circle line in (b).

Vibration modals and corresponding resonance response of ultrasonic sonotrode, showing how the vibration displacement distributes along ultrasonic sonotrode. (a)-(e) indicate the fifth-order modals of vibration at different basic frequencies, and (f) represents the actual vibration of ultrasonic sonotrode at a frequency of 20.387 kHz under service.

Simulated results of acoustic pressure in a 3-D solidification volume of Al alloy melt: (a) the FE model of sonotrode-melt pair; (b1)-(b4) the spatial distribution and evolution of acoustic pressure in one cyclic time. The dashed white circled areas (marked by × in (b)) show boundaries of some intense cavitation zones containing potential dense bubbles. Colours correspond to values of acoustic pressure. (For quality interpretation, the reader is referred to the online colour version of this article and the supplementary movie).
Experimental and simulated vibration amplitudes along the i, ii, iii, and vi lines on the longitudinal surface of ultrasonic sonotrode. Simu in this figure represents simulation.

(a) Superimposition of incident (µ1) and reflecting (µ2) waves on the ultrasonic sonotrode surface. Calculated amplitudes of longitudinal vibration along the i, ii, iii, and vi lines on ultrasonic sonotrode at (b) ambient condition and (c) casting condition.
Experimental and simulated vibration amplitudes along the v and vi lines on the tip surface of ultrasonic sonotrode.
Parameters used for the ultrasound-assisted large-scale casting.
Key parameters used to simulate dynamic behaviour of the acoustic field around ultrasonic sonotrodes [16–18]

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: UTS denotes the ultrasonic transmission velocity in corresponding materials.

head.ce:title: The cavitation erosion of ultrasonic sonotrode during large-scale metallic casting: Experiment and simulation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of High Performance Complex Manufacturing, College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of High Performance Complex Manufacturing
College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: IMDEA Materials Institute, C/Eric Kandel 2, 28906 Getafe, Madrid, Spain

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of High Performance Complex Manufacturing, College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of High Performance Complex Manufacturing
College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrasonic sonotrodes play an essential role in transmitting power ultrasound into the large-scale metallic casting. However, cavitation erosion considerably impairs the in-service performance of ultrasonic sonotrodes, leading to marginal microstructural refinement. In this work, the cavitation erosion behaviour of ultrasonic sonotrodes in large-scale castings was explored using the industry-level experiments of Al alloy cylindrical ingots (i.e. 630 mm in diameter and 6000 mm in length). When introducing power ultrasound, severe cavitation erosion was found to reproducibly occur at some specific positions on ultrasonic sonotrodes. However, there is no cavitation erosion present on the ultrasonic sonotrodes that were not driven by electric generator. Vibratory examination showed cavitation erosion depended on the vibration state of ultrasonic sonotrodes. Moreover, a finite element (FE) model was developed to simulate the evolution and distribution of acoustic pressure in 3-D solidification volume. FE simulation results confirmed that significant dynamic interaction between sonotrodes and melts only happened at some specific positions corresponding to severe cavitation erosion. This work will allow for developing more advanced ultrasonic sonotrodes with better cavitation erosion-resistance, in particular for large-scale castings, from the perspectives of ultrasonic physics and mechanical design.

ce:section.ce:para: Large-scale casting has become an attractive topic due to its potential application in manufacturing large-scale metallic components [1–4]. As a key unit of ultrasound-assisted casting apparatus, ultrasonic sonotrodes play the most critical role in large-scale casting [1,2]. Ultrasonic sonotrodes are usually immersed in the molten metal for a long time during large-scale casting. Through ultrasonic sonotrodes, power ultrasound can be transmitted from the ultrasonic generator into the metallic melts, promoting microstructural refinement during solidification [5–9]. Further, it enhances the corresponding mechanical properties of metallic materials [6,10]. Acoustic cavitation and streaming flow in the metallic melts are two fundamental physical phenomena, which significantly affect solidification in casting [1,2,11–14]. Meanwhile, they are also closely associated with the dynamic interaction between ultrasonic sonotrodes and metallic melts [2,15]. Such interaction considerably decides the in-service performance of ultrasonic sonotrodes in large-scale casting. Thus, the long-time exposure of ultrasonic sonotrodes in the molten metal, in turn, causes their strong degradation. As the major failure mechanism of many ultrasound-driven mechanical parts, cavitation erosion leads to high maintenance costs [15]. Thus, an in-depth investigation of the cavitation erosion behaviour of ultrasonic sonotrodes in actual solidification is essentially required. It facilitates to develop erosion-resistant ultrasonic sonotrodes and to predict their performance.

Currently, a large effort is being mainly devoted to (a) studying the relationship between ultrasonic parameters and microstructural evolution [1,5,8,9], and (b) the physical science of acoustic cavitation [13–15]. Unfortunately, only minor attention was paid to investigate the cavitation erosion behaviour of ultrasonic sonotrodes in large-scale casting [2]. Most recently, our co-workers investigated how the mass-loss of ultrasonic sonotrodes varied at different periods of cavitation erosion during large-scale casting [2]. In fact, their work only focused on the ultrasonic sonotrodes’ tip surface. Meanwhile, the exact physical mechanisms, responsible for the cavitation erosion occurring on ultrasonic sonotrodes, remain still beyond understanding. To reveal relevant mechanisms, it entails answering a few questions. (1) How acoustic pressure distributes and evolves in the 3-D volume of metallic melts? (2) Which positions of ultrasonic sonotrodes become vulnerable to cavitation when being exposed in the high-temperature scenario? (3) What are the boundaries of acoustic pressure field in the 3-D solidification volume? (4) What are the basic vibration modals corresponding to the resonance of ultrasonic sonotrodes? (5) How the ultrasonic vibration amplitudes distribute along the longitudinal and transversal directions of ultrasonic sonotrodes? (6) What is the critical value of acoustic pressure for cavitation erosion in large-scale casting? The present work is aimed at investigating the physical mechanisms behind cavitation erosion of ultrasonic sonotrodes, including experiment and simulation. These questions above are accordingly answered.

The ultrasound-assisted casting system is shown in Fig. 1 , which is specifically used for large-scale metallic solidification. There were two ultrasonic sonotrodes, i.e. (I) and (II) in Fig. 1. Ultrasonic sonotrodes are a kind of titanium alloys with the same chemical compositions listed in Ref. [2]. In order to evaluate the degree of cavitation erosion, the ultrasonic sonotrodes (I) and (II) were driven with and without electric excitation, respectively. The key parameters of casting process are listed in Table 1 . The main experimental material for ultrasound-assisted large-scale casting was 2A14 Al alloy. 2A14 Al alloy, i.e. Al-4Cu-0.6Si-0.5Mg-0.2Zn-0.5Mn-0.1Ti-0.05Ni-0.1 wt% Fe, was molten in a protective atmosphere of argon in a melting system, followed by removing the dross, stirring, degassing and filtering. Combined with the spinning nozzle inert gas flotation (SNIF®) method, a fumeless inline degassing (FILD®) method was applied to refine melts. Along the launder, 2A14 Al alloy melts were then diverted into the ultrasound-assisted casting system (see Fig. 1). During solidification, the cooling water flowed around the crystallizer to chill and solidify a solid ingot. Meanwhile, the water jets were continuously sprayed on the ingot surface until casting was completed. A cylindrical hot-top was utilized to minimize the shrinkage involved. After 4 h semi-continuous solidification, a large-scale cylindrical 2A14 Al alloy ingot, with 630 mm in diameter and 6000 mm in length, was successfully obtained.

Then, in order to examine the microstructures and cavitation erosion, microstructural characterization of selected samples are firstly carried out using an optical microscope (Leica®) equipped with an image analysis software. The optical micrographs of large-scale cylindrical 2A14 Al alloy with and without being treated by power ultrasound (PU) are shown in Fig. 2 . Before capturing optical micrographs, the 2A14 Al alloy metallographic samples were subjected to a Keller solution (1% HF, 1.5% HCl and 2.5 vol% HNO3). To investigate the cavitation erosion of ultrasonic sonotrodes, both ultrasonic sonotrodes (I) and (II) were cyclically immersed into melts at a depth of 280 mm for 36 h. After cavitation erosion test in large-scale casting, the Al alloy residuals present on ultrasonic sonotrodes were slightly etched by 1.5 vol% HCl. Detailed microstructures were further revealed by scanning electron microscope (SEM; TESCAN, MIRA 3 LMH/LMU). SEM was performed at 15 kV/2.8 nA fitted with energy dispersive spectroscope (EDS). Phase identification of potential intermetallics from cavitation erosion were done using X-ray diffraction (XRD) and EDS. XRD was performed in a Rigaku X-2000 diffractometer at 40 kV with Cu Kα radiation (λkα = 1.54 Å).

Power ultrasound was firstly generated by the ultrasonic vibration system that include piezoelectric transducer, amplitude converter, and ultrasonic sonotrodes. Then, it transitions through ultrasonic sonotrode into the large-scale 2A14 Al alloy melts, further leading to a 3-D acoustic filed in melts. The distribution of acoustic field in melts will significantly affect both the grain refinement of Al alloy and the cavitation erosion of ultrasonic sonotrodes. During large-scale casting, two working conditions for ultrasonic sonotrodes, i.e. in and out of molten metal, will be involved. In order to investigate the ultrasonic dynamic behaviour in melts, a finite element (FE) model was employed for simulation. Regarding the two individual working conditions, FE model are used to simulate (a) the evolution of acoustic field, and (b) the cavitation erosion of ultrasonic sonotrodes. The FE model was developed using a software (HyperMesh v12.0), as shown in Fig. 3 . The coupling effects between piezoelectric transducer, acoustic pressure, melts and ultrasonic sonotrode were considered in this FE model. The cylindrical ultrasonic sonotrode, with 50 mm in diameter and 370 mm in length, was made of Ti alloy. Details of such Ti alloy can be found elsewhere [2]. Ultrasonic sonotrodes were placed at the central position ∼280 mm underneath the surface of Al alloy melt. The initial ultrasound wave length was ∼246.8 mm. Key parameters used in this FE model is listed in Table 2 .

To validate the knots and amplitudes of simulated ultrasonic wave along the ultrasonic sonotrode, the initial vibratory should be examined before casting. For initial vibratory examination before casting, the frequency and power of electric generators were set to be 20 ± 0.7 kHz and 1 kW, respectively. A self-developed close-loop controlling unit was equipped on the electric generator. It ran identically in all casting conditions. The vibration input to the molten metal was relatively stable. The vibration amplitudes of ultrasonic sonotrodes were measured using a laser displacement sensor (LK-G5000 Series, KEYENCE®), as shown in Fig. 4 . Each data point was determined for three times to obtain the average value. The vibration amplitude was determined along the longitudinal and transversal directions of ultrasonic sonotrodes. Layout of data points on ultrasonic sonotrodes was schematically indicated in Fig. 4. There are totally 72 points (along i, ii, iii and vi in Fig. 4) selected for data collection. Additionally, 26 points (along v and vi in Fig. 4) were also examined to evaluate the vibration amplitudes on the tip surface of ultrasonic sonotrodes.

Ultrasonic sonotrodes in the present work are particularly used in industry-level experiment for large-scale castings. These ultrasonic sonotrodes promote to induce microstructural refinement of large-scale Al alloy ingots, such as the cylindrical one (630 mm in diameter and 6000 mm in length). It can be seen in Fig. 2 that significant grain refinement occurred after introducing PU through ultrasonic sonotrodes. Compared with the new ultrasonic sonotrodes in Fig. 5 (a), only minor mass loss, instead of cavitation erosion, was observed on the ultrasonic sonotrode surface (see Fig. 5(b)). Moreover, the ultrasonic sonotrode surface became gray. The samples in Fig. 5(b) were not treated by PU. However, severe cavitation erosion appeared on the surfaces of electricity-driven ultrasonic sonotrodes, as shown in Fig. 5(c) and (d). These cavitation erosion positions were also examined using SEM and XRD. SEM micrographs are shown in Fig. 6 (a) and (b). Numerous Al3Ti intermetallic particles were determined at the cavitation erosion positions. Phase identification of such intermetallic particles has been carried out using XRD in Ref. [2]. For brevity, they are not shown here. These cavitation erosion would significantly deteriorate the microstructural refinement in the large-scale Al alloy casting, further suppressing downstream plastic formability. Comparing Fig. 5(b) and (c)/(d), it is clear that cavitation erosion acts as the dominant mechanism responsible for failure of ultrasonic sonotrodes. Cavitation erosion reproducibly occurred at three positions, i.e. ∼42, 134 and 221 mm from the ultrasonic sonotrode tip, while marginal cavitation erosion appeared on other positions. However, it is unclear why the strongest cavitation erosion reproducibly occurred at the three positions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cavitation erosion locations on ultrasonic sonotrodes
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the FE mode in Fig. 3, vibration modals were calculated to determine the natural frequency and vibration mode. When ignoring damping, modal parameters can be linked using Eq. (1).
(1)
( [ K ] -
ω
2
[ M ] ) { φ } = 0

In which u, C, Fo , and t are designated as displacement vector, damping matrix, load vector, and time, respectively [20,21]. The calculated vibration modals and associated resonance response between 19 and 21 kHz are depicted in Fig. 7 . Fifth-order modals of ultrasonic vibration were accordingly obtained, as shown in Fig. 7(a) through (e). When the frequency is 19.612 kHz, vibration dominates in the longitudinal direction. However, in terms of other frequencies, i.e. 19.761, 19.775, 21.912 and 21.914 kHz, vibration modal undergoes mainly in the form of torsional vibration about the longitudinal direction. According to the theory of modal superposition principle [20], the actual vibration of ultrasonic sonotrode at an actual frequency of 20.387 kHz can be superimposed based on the fifth-order modals. Fig. 7(f) demonstrates the resonance response of ultrasonic sonotrode under service in the actual casting. The ultrasonic sonotrode principally introduced longitudinal vibration while torsional vibration alternated vibration mode on the longitudinal surface of ultrasonic sonotrode. In other words, coupling vibration from longitudinal and torsional vibrations also existed on the longitudinal surface of ultrasonic sonotrode during large-scale casting. The maximum vibration displacement reached 14.8 µm.

The acoustic pressure fulfills Helmholtz equation [21–23]. Based on Helmholtz equation, the ultrasonic radiation in the 3-D solidification volume is expressed using Eq. (3).
(3)

represent acoustic pressure, space vector, and number of ultrasound waves, respectively.
r

= [ x , y , z ]
, k = ω/c.
ω
is the angular frequency, and c is the ultrasonic transmission velocity [23]. Assumption in Eq. (3) was made that the Al alloy melts are relatively uniform. Moreover, the acoustic pressure in the domain of
S
should satify the boundry condition below [23].
(4)

∂p/∂nr represents the exterior normal derivative, j = (−1)1/2. At the infinite distance, the acoustic pressure obyes the radiation requirement as follows.
(5)

In Eq. (5), SR defines a domain of spherical surface with a radius of R [21,23]. Mathematic relationship between the acoustic pressure and the exterior normal velocity can be expressed by Eq. (6) [21,23].
(6)
{ p } = [ z ] {
v
n
}
[z] and {vn } are the acoustic impedance matrix and the normal velocity vector on SR , respectively. Regarding the coupling of US and melts in actual casting, the coupling equation is developed as follows:
(7)

]

}
{ 0 }

Definitions of [M], u, p, [C], [K], and F in Eq. (7) have been given in Eqs. (1)–(3). The subscripts, f and s, represent US and melts, respectively. [Mfs ] and [Kfs ] are the coupling generalized mass matrix and the coupling stiffness matrix between sonotrodes and melts, respectively. Based on Eqs. (2)–(7), the distribution of acoustic pressures on ultrasonic sonotrode can be calculated. When ultrasonic sonotrode was stably located at 280 mm below the Al alloy melt surface, the ultrasonic generator started to work. In orde to obtain the spatial acoustic pressure and displacement fields, Ansys® was then applied to simulate the FE model developed using HyperMesh v12.0. Regarding working at a resonance frequency (20.387 kHz) in one cyclic time, simulated results were indicated in Fig. 8 . Fig. 8(a)–(b) demonstrate how acoustic pressure distributes and evolves in a 3-D solidification volume of Al alloy melt during large-scale casting. Gradient colours reflect how the values of acoustic pressure spatially varied in a 3-D solidification volume. The simulated overall acoustic pressure ranges from 0 to |5.78| MPa. The spatial dynamic evolution of acoustic pressure is shown in Movie 1 in the Supplementary files. Fig. 8(b1)–(b4) are a series of images extracted from Movie 1. Along the longitudinal surface of ultrasonic sonotrode, the maximum acoustic pressure was 4.96 MPa. There are three interesting positions on the ultrasonic sonotrode surface (as displayed by h1 , h2 and h3 in Fig. 8(b1)) at which the acoustic pressure reached over 3.21 MPa. The maximum acoustic pressure was 3.27 for h1 , 3.23 for h2 , and 3.21 MPa for h3 . At the ultrasonic sonotrode tip surface, the maximum acoustic pressure was 5.78 MPa. According to other reported results [21,24], ultrasonic cavitation usually occurred on the given positions where the acoustic pressure is ≥1.1 MPa. 1.1 MPa was considered as the threshold value for generating cavitation [24,25]. h1 , h2 and h3 are probably three intense cavitation zones where dense bubbles may nucleate and grow. Cavitation erosion has most trend to happen at h1 , h2 and h3 positions. Thus, 3.21 MPa was considered as a critical value to induce severe cavitation erosion of ultrasonic sonotrode in the present large-scale casting. Actually, the critical value depends on temperature, materials, melts, etc. [22,25].
Movie 1

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparation between ambient and casting conditions
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to experimentally verify the three positions (h1 , h2 and h3 in Fig. 8), the amplitude data along the i, ii, iii, vi, v, and vi lines on ultrasonic sonotrode (displayed in red dashed lines in Fig. 4) were determined using a laser displacement sensor. Through fast fourier transformation [26,27], spectrum analysis of these data were then obtained, as shown in Figs. 9 and 10
. At ambient condition (25 °C), three peaks were determined along the i, ii, iii and vi lines, which agree well with the simulated results (Fig. 9). At casting condition (650 °C), four peaks reproducibly occurred at the specific positions about 39, 134, 235, and 332 mm from the ultrasonic sonotrode tip. 332 mm will not be discussed as it is above the melt surface. The values of four peaks lie in a range of 4.93–5.45 µm. The interpeak spacing is 100 ± 3 mm (∼0.5λTi, λTi represents the ultrasound wavelength when it transmits through the Ti alloy ultrasonic sonotrode). It implies that the ultrasound wavelength decreased when ultrasonic sonotrodes worked at high temperature of 650 °C. This is attributed to superimposition of the incident (µ1) and reflecting (µ2) waves on the ultrasonic sonotrode surface [27,28], as demonstrated in Fig. 10(a).
(8)

=

(9)

A1 and A2 are the vibration amplitudes of µ1 and µ2, t is transmission time, T is one cyclic time, λ is wavelength, and x is transmission length. When µ1 approaches the interface between sonotrode and melt, the ultrasound wave will reflect back to form µ2. Z1 and Z2 represent the characteristic impedances.

are the ultrasonic sonotrode density and the velocity of ultrasonic wave, respectively. Then, after superimposition of µ1 and µ2, the standing wave, µ(x, t), can be given as Eq. (10) [27,28].

. However, at the casting temperature of 650 °C, the wavelength in ultrasonic sonotrode will be alternated due to the variation of physical properties at high temperature [29]. At a given temperature (T), the wavelength in ultrasonic sonotrode is able to be calculated using Eq. (11).
(11)
λ
T
=
1
f
E
T
ρ
T

In which
E
T
and
ρ
T
are termed as the elastic modulus and corresponding mass densities at T, respectively.
E
T
and
ρ
T
can be found elsewhere [30–34]. Actually,
E
T
and
ρ
T
vary with temperature [30,32]. At 650 °C during casting, the wavelength λT was calculated to be 184.6 mm in the present work. The interpeak displacing increased from 123.4 mm at ambient condition (25 °C) to 92.3 mm at casting condition (650 °C). The calculated four wave knots are 1/4 λT = 45 mm, 3/4 λT = 135 mm, 5/4 λT = 225 mm, 7/4 λT = 315 mm (Fig. 10(b) and (c)). These analytical solutions are in accordance with both the experimentally determined and simulated positions for cavitation erosion.

Moreover, the vibration amplitudes along the v and vi lines on the tip surface of ultrasonic sonotrode range from 11.35 to 12.61 mm, Fig. 11 . The maximum vibration amplitude is 12.61 mm at the central point, followed by continuous decrease to 11.35 mm at the edge. The vibration amplitudes along the v and vi lines (on the tip surface of ultrasonic sonotrode) are nearly twice of that along the i, ii, iii, and vi lines (on the longitudinal direction of ultrasonic sonotrode). In the Al alloy melts, cavitation bubbles nucleate once acoustic pressure reaches over the threshold value [35]. After sonication begins, the majority of bubbles may nucleate and grow within in very short time (
≪
78
ms
) [36]. Normally, cavitation bubbles nucleate and grow in the negative acoustic pressure phase, while they will be significantly compressed until collapse in the positive acoustic pressure phase [35]. It was verified that dense bubbles tend to occur nearly at some special zones/positions of ultrasonic sonotrode surface [11]. Compared to the ultrasound-generated bubbles in water, the bubble radii would increase by one order in magnitude in the Al alloy melts [11]. The intense cavitation zone containing dense bubbles appeared at knots of standing waves (i.e. h1 , h2 and h3 positions). Thus, cavitation bubbles near the h1 , h2 and h3 positions may first collapse due to their exposure under very strong ultrasonic vibration, as evidenced by the results in Figs. 8, 9 and 11. When these cavitation bubbles collapsed, some micro-jet flows instantaneously formed near the h1 , h2 and h3 positions. After experiencing a few cyclic time, such micro-jet flows further repeat to impact on the ultrasonic sonotrode surface, resulting in release of Ti atoms to Al alloy melts. The Ti atoms released from ultrasonic sonotrode further interacted with the Al atoms in melts to form small Al3Ti intermetallic particles on the ultrasonic sonotrode surface [2,37]. Brittle Al3Ti intermetallic particles were then peeled off from ultrasonic sonotrode surface to become various fragments. Fragmentation of the intermetallic dendrites have recently been confirmed by synchrotron X-ray radiography in real time [38]. Both acoustic cavitation and streaming flow considerably affect intermetallic fragmentation and/or detachment [38]. During cyclic vibration in large-scale casting, the Al3Ti intermetallic particles would repeatedly develop and leave from ultrasonic sonotrode surface. Thus, the dense materials at the positions (h1 , h2 , h3 and the ultrasonic sonotrode tip) become deteriorated, leading to cavitation erosion (see Fig. 5). The degree of cavitation erosion is closely associated with the distribution of vibration displacements on the ultrasonic sonotrode surface. The positions (h1 , h2 , h3 and the ultrasonic sonotrode tip) experienced the strongest cavitation erosion than any other positions of ultrasonic sonotrode.

Ultrasonic sonotrodes enabled to induce effective grain refinement during the large-scale casting of Al alloys. However, due to the severe cavitation erosion, their in-service performance was significantly impeded after 36 h service at a casting temperature of 650 °C. Such cavitation erosion reproducibly appeared at three positions (h1 , h2 and h3 ), i.e. 42, 134 and 221 mm from the ultrasonic sonotrode tip. The maximum acoustic pressures at these three positions reached 3.27, 3.23 and 3.21 MPa, respectively. The minimum acoustic pressure for sever cavitation erosion was concluded to be 3.21 MPa in large-scale casting. When the incident and reflecting ultrasonic waves superimposed, the standing waves were developed in ultrasonic sonotrode. At ambient temperature of 25 °C, there are only three wave knots present on the longitudinal surface of ultrasonic sonotrode. Four wave knots occurred while ultrasonic sonotrodes retained at 650 °C. Meanwhile, the wavelength was decreased from 123.4 mm at 25 °C to 92.3 mm at 650 °C. The average interpeak spacing on the longitudinal surface of ultrasonic sonotrode is 100 ± 3 mm, which equals to 0.5λTi. Actually, h1 , h2 and h3 were very close to the wave knots. Dense bubbles may nucleate and grow within the intense cavitation zones near h1 , h2 and h3 . Cavitation bubbles at wave knots became very vulnerable due to the high acoustic pressure generated at wave knots. When these cavitation bubbles collapsed, some micro-jet flows instantaneously formed near the wave knots positions. Such micro-jet flows further impacted on the brittle Al3Ti intermetallic particles that formed at the interface between sonotrode and melt. Then, the brittle Al3Ti intermetallic particles were peeled off from ultrasonic sonotrode. After a few cyclic castings, the Al3Ti intermetallic particles would repeatedly form and leave from ultrasonic sonotrode. Finally, the dense materials at h1 , h2 and h3 positions scarified after service. Attention should be paid to these vulnerable positions in terms of ultrasonic sonotrode design.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51605496). Z. Liu acknowledges Comunidad de Madrid through the Atracción de Talento Fellowship. This work is financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (Grant Nos. 51475480, 51575539, U1637601). Y. Tian thanks Central South University Innovation Foundation for Postgraduate (No. 1053320170093). Z. Liu and R. Li further acknowledge financial support from the State Key Laboratory of High Performance Complex Manufacturing through No. ZZYJKT2016-03 and No. ZZYJKT2017-01, respectively.
